description:
You are the configuration manager for a small team.  
Everything you need to look after lives under  

/home/user/configs  

That tree already contains several “.conf” files buried in different sub-directories.  
Your goal is to:  

1. Create a brand-new directory called  
   /home/user/configs_recent_backup  

2. Copy every “.conf” file that has been modified in the last 30 days from
   /home/user/configs to the new backup directory **while preserving the
   original directory hierarchy** (i.e., nginx/nginx.conf must end up at
   /home/user/configs_recent_backup/nginx/nginx.conf, etc.).  
   • Any “.conf” file **older** than 30 days must **NOT** be copied.  

3. When you are done copying, generate a strictly-formatted manifest file named  

   /home/user/configs_recent_backup/backup_manifest.log  

   The manifest must follow this exact layout so that the automated grader can
   recognise it:

   • Line 1 – Header  
     Backup Manifest - YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS  

     • The timestamp must be the current local time of the container when the
       file is written, formatted exactly as shown above (24-hour clock).  

   • Lines 2 … N – One entry per backed-up file, listed in **alphabetical
     (lexicographic) order by relative path**.  
     Each entry must look like this (note the two spaces between checksum and
     path):  

     <64-char-hex-sha256>␠␠<relative/path/from/configs/root>  

     Example (DON’T reuse these values – they are only illustrative):  
     27dd1f61d7f04d2e28593b75892e6b7f9486a1a337af48e2c344b9b4c08021c␠␠app/app.conf  

   • Final line – Footer  
     Total files backed up: X  

     where “X” is the exact count of lines that you wrote in the section above
     (not counting the header or footer).  

   • The file must end with a trailing newline.  

4. While doing all of this, rely solely on standard Unix utilities.
   This is deliberately a “find + xargs” exercise, so those two commands
   should be the core of your solution.

Verification notes for you:
• If the manifest format (header, two-space separator, footer, final newline,
  alphabetical order) deviates in any way, automated grading will fail.  
• Only the “.conf” files **newer than 30 days** may appear in the backup and in
  the manifest. Any older files must be absent from both.  
• The grader will recompute every SHA-256 checksum from the content that ends
  up in the backup directory and compare them to what you wrote in the
  manifest, so make sure the checksums and relative paths are correct.  
• Do not print the manifest to STDOUT; it must be written to the file mentioned
  above. Printing additional progress messages to the console is fine.  
• You do not need (and will not get) elevated privileges – stay in /home/user.  

The task is complete when:
• /home/user/configs_recent_backup exists,  
• it contains only the qualifying “.conf” files in their preserved directory
  layout, and  
• /home/user/configs_recent_backup/backup_manifest.log passes all formatting
  and checksum checks described above.
truth:
Initial file system snapshot (already present before the agent starts):

/home/user/configs/
├── nginx/
│   ├── nginx.conf
│   └── sites-available/
│       └── default.conf
├── app/
│   ├── app.conf
│   └── db/
│       └── database.conf
└── obsolete/
    └── old.conf

File contents (all files end with a trailing newline):
1. nginx/nginx.conf
   user  www-data;
   worker_processes  auto;
   pid /run/nginx.pid;

2. nginx/sites-available/default.conf
   server {
       listen 80 default_server;
       server_name _;
       root /var/www/html;
   }

3. app/app.conf
   [app]
   name = SampleApp
   version = 1.2.3

4. app/db/database.conf
   [database]
   host = localhost
   port = 5432

5. obsolete/old.conf
   deprecated=true

Modification timestamps (relative to container start time):
• nginx.conf, default.conf, app.conf, database.conf : “now – 2 days”  
• obsolete/old.conf                               : “now – 90 days”

Thus:
• The four recent files **must** be backed up.  
• obsolete/old.conf **must not** be copied or appear in the manifest.

Expected backup directory after successful completion:
/home/user/configs_recent_backup/
└── (preserved hierarchy containing only the four recent files)

Expected manifest file:
/home/user/configs_recent_backup/backup_manifest.log

Manifest structural expectations (regex-like):
^Backup Manifest - \d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}\n
^[0-9a-f]{64}  app/app\.conf\n
^[0-9a-f]{64}  app/db/database\.conf\n
^[0-9a-f]{64}  nginx/nginx\.conf\n
^[0-9a-f]{64}  nginx/sites-available/default\.conf\n
^Total files backed up: 4\n\z

The grader will:
• Verify that exactly four files exist under /home/user/configs_recent_backup
  with paths matching those shown above.  
• Recompute each file’s SHA-256 checksum and make sure the manifest line for
  that file matches "<checksum><two spaces><relative path>".  
• Check that the entries are in the alphabetical order shown above.  
• Confirm the header timestamp format and correct footer count.
name: task_000000_d3ad8877
